Hosta.description: Hostas form low mounds of broad leaves ranging from blue-green to chartreuse to variegated white and gold, with tall stalks of pale lavender or white flowers appearing from June into August. They thrive in the shade and damp soil typical of PNW gardens, but slugs are a constant problem here and will shred the leaves if you don't bait or hand-pick. Morning sun is fine for most varieties; the bluer ones hold their color better in deeper shade.

3a.notes: Emerging hosta is slug candy.

3a.product: 4-6 inch straw or shredded leaves over crown
3a.notes: Critical for protecting crown through deep freezes.

3b.notes: Emerging hosta is slug candy.

Garden mum.description: Garden mums are bushy, mounding perennials with deeply lobed green leaves and clusters of daisy-like flowers in shades of bronze, yellow, pink, purple, and white. In the PNW they bloom from late summer into October, though wet autumns can rot the blooms before they fully open. They want full sun and sharp drainage, and most nursery mums sold in fall are bred for show rather than hardiness, so plant them in spring if you want them to reliably return.

3a.notes: PNW rule: pinch monthly until 4th of July. Delays bloom + makes bushy.

Stop when buds form. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
Spring cut-back - leave stems for winter protection.

3a.product: 6-8in straw or leaf mulch over crown
3a.notes: Critical in Zone 3a - mums are marginally hardy; heavy mulch after ground freezes.
After hard freezes pass to prevent crown rot.

Asian lily.description: Asian lilies grow 2-4 feet tall on sturdy stems with narrow, glossy leaves and upward-facing flowers in bold colors like orange, yellow, red, pink, and white. In the Pacific Northwest they bloom in June and early July, earlier than Oriental lilies and without fragrance. Give them full sun and well-drained soil; watch for red lily beetles, which can strip the foliage quickly if you don't pick them off by hand.

Azalea (deciduous).description: Deciduous azaleas are open, twiggy shrubs reaching 4-6 feet, with clusters of trumpet-shaped flowers in shades of orange, yellow, pink, or red that often appear before or with the leaves, which color up nicely in fall. In the Pacific Northwest they bloom from mid-April through May, with some later varieties stretching into June. Give them morning sun with afternoon shade and consistent moisture; powdery mildew on the leaves by late summer is common and mostly cosmetic.

3a.notes: Acidic mulch.

Lighter feeder than evergreen azaleas. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
Never hard-prune — they bloom on old wood.
Less drought tolerant than evergreens; native R. occidentale needs supplemental summer water.
Most deciduous azaleas marginal here; choose Northern Lights series.

Balloon flower.description: Balloon flower (Platycodon grandiflorus) forms a tidy clump of upright stems with star-shaped blue, white, or pink flowers that pop open from puffy balloon-like buds. In the PNW it blooms from late June into August, preferring full sun to light afternoon shade and average, well-drained soil. It's slow to emerge in spring, so mark its spot to avoid digging into the deep, brittle taproot, which makes it hard to move once established.

Blue anise.description: Blue anise sage (Salvia guaranitica) is a tall, upright perennial reaching 4-5 feet, with deep cobalt-blue tubular flowers held above coarse, anise-scented green leaves. In the PNW it blooms from midsummer through the first frost, attracting hummingbirds heavily. It wants full sun and regular water, and in Zone 8 it's root-hardy but dies back to the ground each winter, so mulch the crown and be patient — it's slow to reappear in spring.

3a.notes: Cut in spring, NOT fall - hollow stems protect crown.

Camellia.description: Camellias are broadleaf evergreen shrubs with glossy dark green leaves and rose-like flowers in white, pink, or red, typically reaching 6-12 feet. In the PNW, sasanqua types bloom fall into winter while japonica types flower late winter into spring, which is a welcome stretch when little else is going. They prefer part shade and acidic, well-drained soil; watch for camellia petal blight, which browns the flowers in wet springs, and rake up fallen petals to slow it down.

Canterbury bells.description: Canterbury bells (Campanula medium) is an upright biennial reaching 2-3 feet tall with rosettes of fuzzy leaves the first year and tall spikes of large, cup-shaped bells in pink, blue, white, or purple the second year. In the PNW they bloom from late May into July, preferring full sun on the coast and afternoon shade inland with consistent moisture. Stake the flower stalks before they topple in wet weather, and let a few go to seed if you want them to keep showing up since each plant dies after flowering.

Coneflower.description: Coneflower (Echinacea) is an upright perennial with daisy-like flowers, usually pink-purple with a raised orange-brown central cone, growing two to three feet tall on stiff stems. In the PNW it blooms from July into September, sometimes longer if you deadhead. It wants full sun and well-drained soil; our wet winters can rot the crowns, so avoid heavy clay or soggy spots, and don't bother watering much once established.

Dahlia.description: Dahlias are tuberous perennials with bushy green foliage and showy blooms ranging from small pompoms to dinner-plate sized flowers in nearly every color but blue. In the PNW they bloom from midsummer until the first hard frost, usually October, and want full sun with regular water. Slugs devour young shoots in spring, and the tubers will rot if left in wet winter soil, so many gardeners lift and store them after frost.

Day lily.description: Daylilies form clumps of arching, grass-like foliage with tall scapes carrying trumpet-shaped flowers in nearly every color but true blue and white; each bloom lasts only a day, but a healthy plant opens new ones for weeks. In the PNW they typically bloom June through August, with reblooming varieties stretching into September. They want full sun for best flowering (light afternoon shade is fine), tolerate most soils, and are largely trouble-free aside from occasional aphids on the buds and slugs chewing new spring growth.

Eastern beebalm.description: Eastern beebalm (Monarda bradburiana) is a compact, clump-forming perennial with pale lavender-pink flowers spotted with purple, sitting atop square stems with aromatic mint-family foliage. In the PNW it usually blooms May into June, earlier than the more common Monarda didyma, and goes quietly dormant by late summer. It handles drier soil better than its cousins and shows much better powdery mildew resistance, though it still prefers full sun and decent air circulation.

English lavender.description: English lavender forms a tidy mound of narrow gray-green leaves with slender stems of purple flower spikes, usually about two feet tall and wide. In the PNW it blooms from late June into August, sometimes with a lighter second flush if you shear it after the first round. It needs full sun and sharp drainage — our wet winters will rot it in heavy clay, so amend with grit or plant on a slope.

Foxglove.description: Foxglove is a tall biennial with a rosette of fuzzy leaves the first year and a 3-5 foot spike of tubular, freckled flowers in shades of pink, purple, or white the second year, blooming in the PNW from late May into July. It self-sows readily in part shade and moist soil, so once you have it you tend to keep having it. All parts are toxic if eaten, which is worth knowing if you have curious kids or pets.

Garden phlox.description: Garden phlox (Phlox paniculata) forms upright clumps two to four feet tall, topped with dome-shaped clusters of fragrant pink, purple, white, or magenta flowers from mid-July into September in the PNW. It wants full sun and steady moisture at the roots, but powdery mildew is almost guaranteed if you crowd the plants or wet the foliage—space them well and water at the base. Look for mildew-resistant varieties like 'David' or the Flame series if you've struggled with it before.

Hairy beardtongue.description: Hairy beardtongue (Penstemon hirsutus) is a low, clump-forming perennial with fuzzy stems and narrow green leaves, topped in late spring to early summer with tubular pale lavender flowers that have white throats. It prefers full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and will rot quickly in the wet PNW winters if planted in heavy clay or anywhere water pools. Bees and hummingbirds work it hard while it's in bloom.

Iris.description: Iris are upright perennials with sword-shaped leaves and showy flowers in nearly every color, with three upright petals (standards) and three drooping ones (falls). In the PNW, bearded iris bloom May into June, while Pacific Coast and Siberian types start a bit earlier. They want full sun and good drainage; rhizomes rot if buried too deep or kept too wet, so plant them shallow with the tops just showing.

3a.notes: Bearded iris hates high-N + buried rhizomes. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

Lenten rose.description: Lenten rose (Helleborus orientalis) is an evergreen perennial with leathery, deep green palmate leaves and nodding cup-shaped flowers in shades of white, pink, plum, and green, often speckled inside. In the PNW it blooms from late February into April, sometimes earlier in mild winters, and the old flowers hang on for months as they fade to dusty green. It prefers part to full shade and well-drained soil; cut back tattered old leaves in late winter so the blooms show, and be aware that all parts are toxic if eaten.

Lilac.description: Lilac is a deciduous shrub with heart-shaped leaves and dense clusters of fragrant purple, white, or pink flowers, typically blooming in the PNW from late April into May. It needs full sun and well-drained soil to flower well, and skimping on sun is the main reason gardeners get lots of leaves but few blooms. Powdery mildew often shows up on the foliage by late summer west of the Cascades, but it's cosmetic and doesn't really harm the plant.

Oregon checkerbloom.description: Oregon checkerbloom (Sidalcea oregana) is a slender perennial reaching about 2 to 4 feet tall, with cup-shaped pink mallow flowers stacked along upright spikes above lobed basal leaves. In western Oregon and Washington it blooms from June into August, often longer if you cut back the spent stalks. It likes full sun and moist soil in spring but tolerates summer dryness once established, and rust can disfigure the foliage in damp, crowded conditions.

Panicle hydrangea.description: Panicle hydrangea is a large deciduous shrub with cone-shaped flower clusters that open creamy white in midsummer and often age to pink or rosy red by fall. In the PNW it typically blooms from July into September and is the one hydrangea that genuinely wants sun—at least four to six hours, or it gets floppy and shy with flowers. Prune hard in late winter for bigger blooms, and give it consistent summer water during our dry stretch.

Peony.description: Peonies form bushy clumps about two to three feet tall with deeply cut green leaves and large, often fragrant flowers in white, pink, or red. In the Pacific Northwest they bloom in May and early June, sometimes flopping after rain unless staked or grown as sturdier Itoh or tree types. They want full sun and well-drained soil, and won't bloom if the crown is planted more than an inch or two deep.

Peruvian lily.description: Peruvian lily (Alstroemeria) forms clumps of upright stems with twisted, lance-shaped leaves and clusters of streaked, funnel-shaped flowers in pinks, oranges, yellows, and reds. In the PNW it blooms from June into early fall, especially if you pull (rather than cut) spent stems to encourage rebloom. Give it morning sun with afternoon shade in hotter spots, steady moisture, and a deep mulch in winter; established clumps spread by tuberous roots and can be hard to move once settled.

Poppy anemone.description: Poppy anemone (Anemone coronaria) is a low, ferny-leaved tuberous perennial with bright cup-shaped flowers in red, purple, pink, or white, each with a dark central boss of stamens. In the PNW it typically blooms from April into June if planted in fall, and again lightly in early fall from spring plantings. Give it full sun to light shade and well-drained soil — tubers rot easily in our wet winters, so heavy clay or soggy spots will kill them.

Rhododendron.description: Rhododendrons are broadleaf evergreen shrubs with leathery oval leaves and rounded clusters of pink, purple, white, or red flowers, typically blooming April through May in the Pacific Northwest. They prefer dappled shade or morning sun with afternoon shade, and need acidic, well-draining soil since they hate wet feet. Watch for root weevils notching the leaves and for yellowing foliage, which usually means the soil is too alkaline or too soggy.

Rose lily.description: Rose lily is a double-flowered Oriental lily with ruffled, peony-like blooms in shades of pink, red, or white, growing on stems about three to four feet tall. In the Pacific Northwest it blooms from mid to late summer, usually July into August, and the flowers are pollen-free, which is handy if you want to bring them indoors. Give it full sun to light afternoon shade and well-drained soil; watch for lily beetles and don't let the bulbs sit in winter wet, or they'll rot.

Scarlet beebalm.description: Scarlet beebalm (Monarda didyma) is an upright perennial reaching 3-4 feet with shaggy, bright red tubular flowers crowning square stems and aromatic mint-family foliage. In the Pacific Northwest it blooms from July into August and pulls in hummingbirds reliably. It prefers full sun and consistent moisture, but powdery mildew is a near-certainty here — give it good airflow and consider mildew-resistant cultivars like 'Jacob Cline'.

Scarlet sage.description: Scarlet sage (Salvia splendens) is a bushy annual reaching 1–2 feet, with bright green oval leaves and dense spikes of vivid red tubular flowers that hummingbirds love. In the PNW it typically blooms from late June through the first hard frost in October. It wants full sun and consistent moisture; in our cool springs, wait until nights are reliably above 50°F before planting out, or growth will stall and leaves may yellow.

Sea thrift.description: Sea thrift forms tidy evergreen tufts of grassy, dark green foliage topped with round pink (sometimes white) flower heads on slender bare stems, blooming heavily from April into June in the PNW with sporadic reflushes through summer. It wants full sun and sharp drainage; soggy winter soil or rich, mulched beds will rot the crown out. The center of the clump tends to die out after a few years, so lift and divide every three or four springs to keep it looking full.

Showy lily.description: Showy lily (Lilium speciosum) is a tall stem lily, usually 3-5 feet, with broad recurved petals in white flushed with deep pink and dark raspberry spots. It's a late bloomer for a lily, opening in August and September, which makes it useful for stretching the season after Asiatics and orientals fade. Give it morning sun with afternoon shade in the PNW, sharp drainage, and watch for lily leaf beetle and botrytis in wet springs.

Snapdragon.description: Snapdragons send up spikes of hinged, two-lipped flowers in colors ranging from white and yellow to pink, red, and burgundy, usually on plants 1 to 3 feet tall. In the PNW they bloom from late spring through fall, and in milder parts of Zone 8 they often overwinter and return for a second year. They prefer full sun and decent drainage; rust is the main headache, so give them airflow and avoid wetting the foliage.

3a.notes:
Watch for: Disease (leaf spot/root rot)
Star jasmine is not winter-hardy in Zone 3a and must be grown in a container brought indoors. Check foliage for fungal spotting as it moves outside; ensure good airflow and avoid overhead watering.
Added for cold zone: wait until well after last frost (around Memorial Day in 3a) and night temps stay above 50°F. Harden off over 7-10 days to prevent leaf scorch.
Feed an established vine during the short growing season with an organic acid-loving or flower-promoting fertilizer. Scratch into the soil at the dripline and water in well.
Water deeply once a week during summer, more often for vines in containers. Star jasmine tolerates some drought once established but blooms and looks best with consistent moisture.

Watch for: pests
Inspect new growth and leaf undersides for aphids, scale, mealybugs, and spider mites. Spot-treat with insecticidal soap or a strong water spray; repeat in 7-10 days if needed.

3a.task: Tie in and train new growth
3a.notes: Star jasmine twines but needs help getting started on trellises, wires, or arbors. Guide and loosely tie new shoots monthly during the growing season.
Prune right after the main flush of bloom finishes to shape the vine and control size without sacrificing next year's flowers. Remove dead, tangled, or wayward stems and tie new growth to supports.
Added for cold zone: bring the vine inside well before first frost (mid-September in 3a). Overwinter in a bright cool room (50-60°F) and reduce watering.

3b.notes:

7a.notes: For overgrown or bare-bottomed vines, do a harder renewal cut just before spring growth resumes, after the worst frost risk has passed. You'll sacrifice some of that year's bloom but gain a denser, healthier plant.

Watch for: Disease (leaf spot/root rot)
Spring rains can bring fungal leaf spot; ensure good airflow and avoid overhead watering. If spotting appears, remove affected leaves and apply copper fungicide; check that drainage is sharp to prevent root rot.

7a.product: Pine Bark Mulch or Local Compost [(buy)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07Q5JF8VN)
7a.notes: Apply 3-4 inches of bark or compost mulch over the root zone, keeping it a few inches off the main stem. Heavier mulch is critical in Zone 7a to insulate roots through cold winters.
Feed an established vine three times during the growing season with an organic acid-loving or flower-promoting fertilizer. Scratch into the soil at the dripline and water in well.

Watch for: pests
Inspect new growth and leaf undersides for aphids, scale, mealybugs, and spider mites — most common in warm, sheltered spots. Spot-treat with insecticidal soap or a strong water spray; repeat in 7-10 days if needed.

Watch for: Iron/chlorosis
Soils can pH-creep alkaline near foundations and concrete, causing yellow leaves with green veins. If you see chlorosis, apply chelated iron and consider an acidifying fertilizer going forward.

7a.task: Tie in and train new growth
7a.notes: Star jasmine twines but needs help getting started on trellises, wires, or arbors. Guide and loosely tie new shoots monthly during the growing season to build a full, even framework.

Water deeply once a week during summer dry spells, more often for vines in containers or against hot south/west walls. Star jasmine tolerates some drought once established but blooms and looks best with consistent moisture.
Prune right after the main flush of bloom finishes to shape the vine and control size without sacrificing next year's flowers. Remove dead, tangled, or wayward stems and tie new growth to supports.

Star jasmine is marginal in 7a and will likely die back below 10°F. Mulch heavily over the roots (6+ inches) and drape frost cloth (not plastic) over the vine when hard freezes are forecast. Plant in a south-facing sheltered microclimate.

Watch for: Disease (leaf spot/root rot)
Wet PNW springs can bring fungal leaf spot; ensure good airflow and avoid overhead watering. If spotting appears, remove affected leaves and apply copper fungicide; check that drainage is sharp to prevent root rot.

For overgrown or bare-bottomed vines, do a harder renewal cut just before spring growth resumes, after the worst frost risk has passed. You'll sacrifice some of that year's bloom but gain a denser, healthier plant.

9a.notes: For overgrown or bare-bottomed vines, do a harder renewal cut just before spring growth resumes. You'll sacrifice some of that year's bloom but gain a denser, healthier plant.

9a.product: Pine Bark Mulch or Local Compost [(buy)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07Q5JF8VN)
9a.notes: Apply 2-3 inches of bark or compost mulch over the root zone, keeping it a few inches off the main stem. This conserves summer moisture in hot climates.
Feed an established vine during the long growing season with an organic acid-loving or flower-promoting fertilizer. Scratch into the soil at the dripline and water in well.

Watch for: pests
Inspect new growth and leaf undersides for aphids, scale, mealybugs, and spider mites — most common in warm, sheltered spots. Spot-treat with insecticidal soap or a strong water spray; repeat in 7-10 days if needed.

Watch for: Iron/chlorosis
Soils can pH-creep alkaline near foundations and concrete, causing yellow leaves with green veins. If you see chlorosis, apply chelated iron and consider an acidifying fertilizer going forward.

9a.task: Tie in and train new growth
9a.notes: Star jasmine twines but needs help getting started on trellises, wires, or arbors. Guide and loosely tie new shoots monthly during the long growing season.

Water deeply once a week during summer, more often for vines in containers or against hot south/west walls. Star jasmine tolerates some drought once established but blooms and looks best with consistent moisture.
Prune right after the main flush of bloom finishes to shape the vine and control size without sacrificing next year's flowers. Remove dead, tangled, or wayward stems and tie new growth to supports.
Star jasmine is fully hardy in 9a but can suffer leaf damage in unusual freezes. Drape frost cloth (not plastic) over the vine when hard freezes are forecast.

Tiger lily.description: Tiger lily (Lilium lancifolium) grows 3–5 feet tall with arching stems, narrow leaves, and downward-facing orange flowers heavily spotted with black, blooming in mid to late summer in the PNW. It prefers full sun to part shade with moist, well-drained soil, and produces small dark bulbils in the leaf axils that drop and sprout, so it can spread more than you expect. Note that it's a known carrier of lily mosaic virus, which it tolerates but can pass to other lilies, and the pollen is highly toxic to cats.

3a.notes: Same as other lilies. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
Heavy mulch critical for bulb survival.

Tube clematis.description: Tube clematis (Clematis heracleifolia) is a shrubby, non-climbing perennial with coarse, three-part leaves and clusters of small, hyacinth-like blue flowers that are lightly fragrant. In the PNW it blooms late summer into early fall, usually August through September. It prefers sun to part shade with steady moisture, and it tends to flop, so cut it back hard in late winter and consider staking or letting it lean on a neighbor.

Clematis likes alkaline. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

Yarrow.description: Yarrow forms a low mat of feathery, fern-like leaves with flat clusters of small flowers on stems 2-3 feet tall, typically white but also available in yellow, pink, and red. It blooms from June into September in the PNW and reblooms if you cut the spent stalks. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil; rich soil or too much water makes it flop and spread aggressively.

Zigzag spiderwort.description: Zigzag spiderwort (Tradescantia subaspera) is a leafy perennial about 2-3 feet tall with a distinctly zigzagging stem and small clusters of three-petaled blue-violet flowers that open in the morning and close by afternoon. In the PNW it blooms from late June into August, preferring part shade and consistently moist soil; full sun tends to scorch the leaves here. The foliage can look ragged by late summer, so cutting it back hard after flowering usually brings a tidier flush of new growth.

3a.notes: Plant looks tired post-bloom; cutting back can trigger fresh foliage.

Hardy hibiscus.description: Hardy hibiscus is a large herbaceous perennial that dies back to the ground each winter and produces dinner-plate-sized flowers in white, pink, or red on stems three to six feet tall. In the PNW it's late to emerge in spring (often not until May) and blooms from late July into September, so mark its spot to avoid digging it up. It needs full sun and consistent moisture to flower well, and Japanese beetles and aphids can chew up the leaves.

3a.product: DO NOT DIG — last perennial to wake up
3a.notes: Mark location in fall so you don't think it's dead in spring.

3a.product: Cut last year's stems to 4–6 inches
3a.notes: Spring cut ONLY — hollow stems shelter crown overwinter.

Hibiscus is hungry; needs spring + midsummer feeds. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
Big leaves and dinner-plate flowers need fuel.
Bog-loving plant; keep consistently moist.
Crown protection through extreme freezes; consider burlap windbreak.

Lupin.description: Lupins form clumps of palmate, fingered leaves with tall spikes of pea-like flowers in blues, purples, pinks, yellows, or bicolors, blooming in the PNW from late May into June. They prefer full sun and well-drained, slightly acidic soil, and tend to be short-lived perennials that often peter out after three or four years. Watch for aphids, which can swarm the flower spikes, and cut spent stalks back to encourage a smaller second flush.

3a.notes: Slugs adore new lupin growth.

Don't over-feed — lupins are nitrogen-fixers. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

3a.product: Hose off or insecticidal soap on flower spikes
3a.notes:
Watch for: Aphid
Aphids cluster on developing spikes.
Encourages a second flush of bloom.

3a.product: Foliage to ground after seed pods drop
3a.notes: Leave a few pods if you want self-sowing.

3a.notes: Critical insulation against extreme cold and freeze-thaw.

Azalea (evergreen).description: Evergreen azaleas are compact, rounded shrubs with small leathery leaves and dense clusters of funnel-shaped flowers in pinks, reds, whites, or purples. In the PNW they typically bloom from April into May, with occasional sparse reblooming in fall. They prefer dappled shade and acidic, well-drained soil; root weevils are the most common headache here, leaving notched leaves and sometimes weakening the plant over time.

3a.notes: Acidic mulch only — no alkaline materials.

Use acid-specific fertilizer, never general-purpose. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
Within 3 weeks of flower drop.
Prune only right after bloom — never late summer/fall (next year's buds set then).
Shallow roots — don't let them dry out in PNW summer drought.

3a.product: 6+ inches pine needles/bark over root zone
3a.notes: Critical insulation; evergreen azaleas marginal here — consider burlap windscreen.
Protects evergreen leaves from winter burn/desiccation.

Bleeding Heart.description: Bleeding Heart (Dicentra spectabilis) has arching stems hung with row after row of pink, heart-shaped flowers above soft, fern-like blue-green foliage. In the PNW it blooms from April into June, then typically dies back and disappears entirely by midsummer — don't panic, it's just dormant and will return next spring. Give it part shade and consistent moisture; mark its spot so you don't dig into the crown once it vanishes.

3a.notes: Apply as new shoots emerge. Bleeding Heart is a light feeder; one spring application plus compost is usually sufficient. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

3a.product: Compost or leaf mold, 2 inch layer
3a.notes: Keeps roots cool and moist through summer. Keep mulch away from crown to prevent rot.
Slugs devour emerging shoots in wet spring. Reapply after heavy rain.

3a.product: Soaker hose or hand water at base
3a.notes: Dicentra prefers consistently moist soil. Water deeply once a week if no rain — plant may go dormant early if too dry.
Foliage naturally yellows and dies back in mid-late summer — this is normal, not disease. Cut to ground once fully brown; mark the spot so you don't dig into the crown.
Divide every 3-4 years if clump declines. Must be done early enough to root before hard freeze; replant immediately and water in.

3a.product: Straw or shredded leaves, 4-6 inch layer over crown
3a.notes: Critical insulation against extreme winter cold and frost heave in Zone 3a. Remove gradually in spring as shoots emerge.

3b.notes: Apply as new shoots emerge. Bleeding Heart is a light feeder; one spring application plus compost is usually sufficient. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

3b.product: Compost or leaf mold, 2 inch layer

Hardy fuchsia.description: Hardy fuchsia is a deciduous shrub with arching stems and dangling, two-toned flowers in shades of red, purple, and pink, usually reaching 3-5 feet in our climate. In the PNW it blooms from June until the first hard frost, feeding hummingbirds well into fall. It prefers part shade with regular summer water, and you should leave the dead stems standing through winter and cut it back to the ground in spring once new growth appears at the base.

3a.product: Old stems to ground when new buds swell
3a.notes: Spring only — NEVER cut in fall in PNW. Old wood protects crown.

Start of growing-season feeding. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
Steady feeder while blooming.
More branching = more flowers.

Watch for: Gall mite
Cut off + bag any infested tips. Fuchsia gall mite is common in PNW — don't compost trimmings.

3a.product: Deep mulch (8-12in) over crown + leaf cover
3a.notes: Zone 3a is at/beyond hardy fuchsia survival range — heavy insulation critical; consider container culture and overwintering indoors.

3a.product: Move potted plants to unheated garage/basement (35-45F)
3a.notes: In-ground survival unreliable below Zone 6 — treat as container plant in Zone 3a.

Hydrangea (big leaf).description: Bigleaf hydrangea is a rounded deciduous shrub, usually 3-6 feet tall, with large serrated leaves and showy mophead or lacecap flower clusters in pink, blue, or purple depending on soil pH—more acidic PNW soils tend to push them blue. They bloom from June into late summer here, and prefer morning sun with afternoon shade plus consistent moisture; the big leaves wilt dramatically on hot days. Most bloom on old wood, so prune lightly right after flowering and avoid cutting back in spring or you'll lose the show.

3a.notes: Affects bloom color via soil pH. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

3a.product: 1 tbsp per gallon water, drench around base
3a.notes: FOR BLUE blooms. Repeat in fall. Skip if you want pink.

3a.product: Cut spent blooms + dead wood; do NOT cut live stems
3a.notes: CRITICAL: blooms on OLD wood. Fall/spring pruning removes next year's buds.
Second dose to keep color.

3a.product: Burlap wrap + 12in mulch mound + leaf-filled cage over crown
3a.notes: Bigleaf hydrangea is marginal here; buds freeze without heavy protection. Expect dieback even so.

Ranunculus.description: Ranunculus produces layered, rose-like blooms in saturated shades of pink, orange, yellow, red, and white on wiry stems above ferny foliage. In the PNW they bloom from late April into June if corms are soaked and planted out in February or March under a cloche; they sulk in heat and usually finish once temperatures climb past the mid-70s. They want full sun and sharp drainage, and the corms rot easily in our wet winter soil, so most gardeners here lift them or treat them as annuals.

3a.product: Soak 3–4 hours, plant 2" deep, claws down
3a.notes: Zone 3a too cold for fall planting — spring plant only after hard frost risk passes. Treat as annual.

Don't over-feed — encourages leaves over flowers. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
Extends the bloom window significantly.
Lift before first hard frost — corms will not survive Zone 3 winters in-ground.

Rose.description: Roses are woody shrubs with thorny canes and compound leaves, producing fragrant flowers in nearly every color except true blue, typically blooming from late May through October in the PNW with a peak in June. They want at least six hours of sun and good air circulation, since our damp springs make black spot and powdery mildew almost inevitable on susceptible varieties. Water deeply at the base rather than overhead, and choose disease-resistant types like rugosas or many David Austins if you'd rather not spray.

3a.product: Cut to outward-facing bud, ~12-18in hybrid teas, lighter for shrubs
3a.notes: PNW rule: when forsythia blooms. Removes winter damage + sets shape.

Stop late summer to let canes harden before winter. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

crepe myrtle.description: Crepe myrtle is a small deciduous tree with smooth, mottled bark and clusters of crinkled pink, white, red, or purple flowers, typically blooming July through September in your area. It needs the hottest, sunniest spot you have to bloom well, since PNW summers can be marginal—shade or cool sites lead to powdery mildew and few flowers. Choose newer mildew-resistant varieties like the 'Indian Tribe' series (Tuscarora, Natchez, Zuni) for better results in your damp climate.

3a.notes: Crepe myrtle is not winter-hardy in Zone 3a — grow only as a container plant overwintered indoors in a cool dormant location (35-45°F garage/basement). In-ground planting will not survive.
Prune in late winter while dormant — remove crossing branches and pencil-thin twigs, but avoid 'crepe murder' (topping). Crepe myrtles bloom on new wood, so light shaping encourages better blooms.

3a.product: Balanced slow-release like 10-10-10 or rose food
3a.notes: Apply when buds begin to swell — typically after moving container outdoors post-frost. Avoid late-summer feeding which can spur tender growth.
Container-grown plants need more frequent attention than in-ground; check soil moisture and water deeply when top inch is dry.

3a.product: Neem oil or potassium bicarbonate spray
3a.notes:
Watch for: powdery mildew
Choose mildew-resistant cultivars (Lagerstroemia indica × fauriei hybrids like 'Natchez', 'Tuscarora') and ensure good air circulation.
Bring container inside before first hard frost. Store in unheated garage or basement; water lightly once a month during dormancy.

3b.notes: Crepe myrtle is not winter-hardy in Zone 3b — grow only as a container plant overwintered indoors in a cool dormant location. In-ground planting will not survive.
Prune in late winter while dormant — remove crossing branches and pencil-thin twigs, but avoid 'crepe murder' (topping). Crepe myrtles bloom on new wood, so light shaping encourages better blooms.

3b.product: Balanced slow-release like 10-10-10 or rose food
3b.notes: Apply when buds begin to swell — typically after moving container outdoors post-frost. Avoid late-summer feeding which can spur tender growth.
Container-grown plants need more frequent attention; check soil moisture and water deeply when top inch is dry.

3b.product: Neem oil or potassium bicarbonate spray
3b.notes:
Watch for: powdery mildew
Choose mildew-resistant cultivars (Lagerstroemia indica × fauriei hybrids like 'Natchez', 'Tuscarora') and ensure good air circulation.
Bring container inside before first hard frost. Store in unheated garage or basement; water lightly once a month during dormancy.

4a.notes: Zone 4a is too cold for in-ground crepe myrtle — grow as a container plant moved indoors for winter, or treat as a die-back shrub if attempting in-ground with heavy mulching of select hardy cultivars like 'Pocomoke'.
Prune while dormant — for die-back culture, cut to ground in spring and let resprout. Crepe myrtles bloom on new wood.

4a.product: Balanced slow-release like 10-10-10 or rose food
4a.notes: Apply when buds begin to swell. Avoid late-summer feeding which can spur tender growth vulnerable to early fall freezes.

4a.product: 2-3 inches of bark mulch or compost
4a.notes: Keep mulch 3 inches from the trunk and consider planting on a mound if drainage is poor.
Established crepe myrtles are drought-tolerant — water deeply but infrequently.

4a.product: Neem oil or potassium bicarbonate spray
4a.notes:
Watch for: powdery mildew
Choose mildew-resistant cultivars (Lagerstroemia indica × fauriei hybrids like 'Natchez', 'Tuscarora') and ensure good air circulation.

4a.product: Heavy mulch (6-12 inches) plus burlap wrap
4a.notes: Tops will die back to the ground. Mound 6-12 inches of mulch over crown to protect roots, and wrap container plants or move inside.

4b.notes: Zone 4b is marginal — only ultra-hardy dwarf cultivars ('Pocomoke', 'Cherry Dazzle') may survive as die-back shrubs with heavy mulching. Container culture is more reliable.
Prune while dormant — for die-back culture, cut to ground in spring and let resprout. Crepe myrtles bloom on new wood.

4b.product: Balanced slow-release like 10-10-10 or rose food

Established crepe myrtles are drought-tolerant — water deeply but infrequently.

4b.product: Neem oil or potassium bicarbonate spray
4b.notes:
Watch for: powdery mildew
Choose mildew-resistant cultivars (Lagerstroemia indica × fauriei hybrids like 'Natchez', 'Tuscarora') and ensure good air circulation.

4b.product: Heavy mulch (6-12 inches) plus burlap wrap
4b.notes: Tops will die back to the ground. Mound 6-12 inches of mulch over crown to protect roots; expect complete top dieback.

5a.notes: Prune in late winter while dormant — remove crossing branches and pencil-thin twigs. In this zone often grown as die-back shrub; cut back hard if winterkilled. Crepe myrtles bloom on new wood.

crocosmia.description: Crocosmia grows from corms into sword-shaped green leaves about 2-3 feet tall, sending up arching stems of fiery red, orange, or yellow tubular flowers from July into August in your area. It prefers full sun and tolerates your wet winters fine once the corms are established, but the clumps spread aggressively and can become a weedy patch within a few years if you don't divide them. Slugs mostly leave the foliage alone, which is a nice bonus.

3a.notes: Plant 3-4 inches deep in clusters once soil warms. In heavy clay, amend planting hole with compost and grit to prevent corm rot in wet winters. Treat as annual or lift corms in fall — not reliably hardy here.
Slugs shred emerging strap leaves. Reapply after heavy rain — pet and wildlife safe.
Avoid high-nitrogen feeds which produce floppy foliage at the expense of blooms. Side-dress lightly around clumps.
Short season — keep watered through bud and bloom. One deep soak per week is better than frequent shallow watering.
Cut individual flower stems at the base once finished to tidy clumps. Leave seed heads on 'Lucifer' if you enjoy the fall structure.

3a.product: Garden fork, dry peat or vermiculite, paper bags
3a.notes: Corms will not survive Zone 3a ground freeze. Lift after first light frost yellows foliage, cure 1 week, store dry at 40-50°F until spring replanting.

3b.notes: Plant 3-4 inches deep in clusters once soil warms. In heavy clay, amend planting hole with compost and grit to prevent corm rot in wet winters. Treat as annual or lift corms in fall — not reliably hardy here.

rose of sharon (Hibiscus syriacus).description: Rose of Sharon is an upright deciduous shrub reaching 8-12 feet tall, with hibiscus-style flowers in white, pink, lavender, or blue, often with a contrasting throat. In your zone it blooms late, usually July into September, leafing out very slowly in spring—don't assume it's dead until June. It tolerates clay and handles dry summers once established, but aphids and Japanese beetles can be a nuisance, and it seeds around prolifically unless you choose a sterile cultivar.

3a.notes: Prune in late winter before bud break to shape and encourage larger blooms. Cut back previous year's growth by 1/3 to 2/3; flowers form on new wood.
Apply after pruning when new growth emerges. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers which promote leaves over flowers.

3a.product: Bark mulch or arborist chips (2-3 inches)
3a.notes: Mulch helps retain moisture through PNW dry summers and improves heavy clay soil over time. Keep mulch 2-3 inches away from the trunk.

3a.product: Soaker hose or slow hose drip
3a.notes: Essential during dry PNW summers, especially for young shrubs (first 2-3 years). Established plants are drought-tolerant but bloom better with consistent moisture.

3a.product: Strong water spray or insecticidal soap
3a.notes:
Watch for: Aphid
Aphids love new growth on Rose of Sharon. Blast with water first; reserve soap sprays for heavy infestations and apply in cool morning hours.

3a.product: Burlap wrap and heavy mulch (6+ inches) over root zone
3a.notes: Zone 3a is at/below the cold hardiness limit for Hibiscus syriacus. Heavy winter protection is essential and survival is not guaranteed; consider container culture brought into an unheated garage.

Aphids love new growth on Rose of Sharon. Blast with water first; reserve soap sprays for heavy infestations and apply in cool morning hours.
Removing spent blooms prevents heavy seed set and reduces volunteer seedlings. Also prolongs the blooming window.
Pull volunteer seedlings while soil is moist and they're easy to extract.
Essential in this zone — site in afternoon shade or rig shade cloth to prevent bud drop, leaf scorch, and shortened bloom.

daphne odora.description: Daphne odora is a compact evergreen shrub, around 3-4 feet tall and wide, with glossy dark leaves and clusters of pink-blushed white flowers that bloom in late winter to early spring (February to March) with a strong sweet fragrance. It prefers part shade and absolutely needs sharp drainage, so amend heavy clay with grit or plant on a slope, since wet feet in winter is the main thing that kills it. Daphnes also dislike being moved or fussed with, so pick the spot carefully and leave it alone.

3a.product: Visual inspection; remove affected branches with sterilized pruners
3a.notes:
Watch for: dieback / sudden decline
Daphne odora is not winter-hardy in Zone 3a and can only be grown as a container plant overwintered indoors in a bright, cool (40-50°F) room. Watch for spider mites and root rot in indoor conditions.

3a.product: Compost top-dress or balanced slow-release (e.g., Osmocote 14-14-14)
3a.notes: Feed lightly just as bloom finishes. Avoid high-nitrogen lawn fertilizers near daphne — they encourage soft growth and sudden death.

3a.product: Fine bark or leaf mold (2 inches, kept off the stem)
3a.notes: Daphne hates wet feet and disturbed roots, so mulch instead of cultivating. Keep mulch pulled back 2-3 inches from the trunk to prevent collar rot.
Prune right after flowering — daphne resents heavy cuts and sets next year's buds on new growth. Remove only dead, crossing, or wayward stems; never cut into old wood.

3a.product: Soaker hose or slow hand-watering at base
3a.notes: Water deeply but infrequently, and never let it sit in saturated soil. Reduce as you prepare to move container indoors.
Move inside well before first hard frost. Daphne odora cannot survive Zone 3a winters outdoors; even one night below 0°F will kill it.

3a.product: N/A — observe and deadhead spent clusters gently

Hardy begonia.description: Hardy begonia (Begonia grandis) is an upright perennial with angel-wing-shaped green leaves backed in red veining, topped with dangling pink flowers from mid-July into October in your area. It prefers part to full shade with consistent summer water, and dies back completely after the first hard freeze before reemerging late in spring—don't panic and dig it up. Slugs will nibble young shoots, so protect the crown when growth resumes in May.

3a.product: Begonia grandis tubers in compost-amended soil
3a.notes: Wait until soil warms — these are slow to emerge in our cool PNW springs, often not appearing until late May or June. Amend heavy clay with compost for drainage; site in part shade with morning sun. Note: Zone 3a is below the reliable hardiness range — treat as an annual or lift tubers for indoor winter storage.
Slugs devour emerging shoots in spring — apply preventively before you see growth. Reapply after heavy rain, which is frequent here through May.

3a.product: Deep soak with soaker hose or drip
3a.notes: Critical during our dry PNW summers — hardy begonia wilts dramatically without consistent moisture. Water deeply at the base; avoid wetting foliage to prevent powdery mildew.

3a.product: Balanced organic fertilizer (e.g., E.B. Stone All Purpose 5-5-5)
3a.notes: Light feeder — don't overdo it. Start once foliage is well established; stop by mid-August so plant can prepare for dormancy. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

3a.product: Peat moss or vermiculite in ventilated container
3a.notes: Zone 3a is too cold for in-ground overwintering. Dig tubers after first light frost, cure briefly, and store in a cool (35-45°F) dry location until spring replanting.
Cut stems to the ground after first hard frost blackens foliage. Remove debris to reduce slug overwintering sites in our wet conditions.

3b.notes: Wait until soil warms — these are slow to emerge in our cool PNW springs, often not appearing until late May or June. 4a.notes: Light feeder — don't overdo it. Start once foliage is well established; stop by mid-August so plant can prepare for dormancy. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
Tiny bulbils form in leaf axils in late summer/fall — collect to propagate or let drop to naturalize. They sprout readily in our climate and form colonies over time.
Cut stems to the ground after first hard frost blackens foliage. Remove debris to reduce slug overwintering sites in our wet conditions.

4b.product: Begonia grandis tubers in compost-amended soil
4b.notes: Wait until soil warms — these are slow to emerge in our cool PNW springs, often not appearing until late May or June. Amend heavy clay with compost for drainage; site in part shade with morning sun. Marginal hardiness in Zone 4b — apply very heavy winter mulch.
Slugs devour emerging shoots in spring — apply preventively before you see growth. Reapply after heavy rain, which is frequent here through May.

4b.product: 4-6 inches of bark mulch or leaf mold
4b.notes: Although hardy to Zone 6, mulching protects tubers during our occasional hard freezes and arctic blasts. Keep mulch slightly away from the crown to prevent rot in wet winters. Double depth for Zone 4b winter protection.

Hollyhock.description: Hollyhocks send up tall spikes, often 5 to 8 feet, lined with papery saucer-shaped flowers in pinks, reds, whites, or near-blacks, blooming June through August in your area. Give them full sun and decent drainage, since our wet clay winters can rot the crowns. Rust is almost guaranteed here—strip affected lower leaves as they appear, and don't bother spraying; the plants usually bloom fine anyway.

3a.notes: PNW slugs devour emerging hollyhock rosettes in spring. Reapply after heavy rain; protect young foliage especially in clay soils that stay moist.

3a.product: Remove infected leaves; apply copper fungicide or Bonide Sulfur Plant Fungicide
3a.notes:
Watch for: Rust
Hollyhock rust is nearly inevitable in our wet PNW springs. Strip lower leaves, water at the base only, and ensure good airflow to slow the spread.

3a.product: Balanced organic fertilizer like E.B. Stone 5-5-5
3a.notes: Hollyhocks are heavy feeders. Avoid high-nitrogen blends that promote lush growth more susceptible to rust.

3a.product: Bamboo stakes or metal plant supports with soft ties
3a.notes: Stalks can reach 6-8 feet and topple in PNW wind/rain storms. Stake before they exceed 3 feet to avoid breaking the taproot.

3a.product: Soaker hose or drip irrigation at base
3a.notes: During our dry summers, water deeply once a week rather than shallow frequent watering. Keep foliage dry to minimize rust.
Extends bloom time and prevents excessive self-seeding. Leave a few late blooms to set seed if you want volunteers next year.
Cut stalks to 6 inches and remove ALL infected foliage from area — do not compost. Sanitation is the best defense against rust returning next year.
Heavy clay + wet winters can rot crowns. Top-dress with 2 inches of compost to improve drainage and insulate from hard freezes.
Apply 6-8 inches over crowns once ground starts to freeze. Zone 3a winters at -40°F will kill unprotected crowns; remove gradually in spring.

3b.notes: PNW slugs devour emerging hollyhock rosettes in spring. Reapply after heavy rain; protect young foliage especially in clay soils that stay moist.

Bearded iris.description: Bearded iris forms fans of upright, sword-shaped gray-green leaves and sends up 2-3 foot stalks of ruffled flowers in nearly every color, blooming here from mid-May into June. Plant the rhizomes shallow with their tops exposed, since they rot quickly in our wet winters and heavy clay—a raised bed or sunny slope works much better than a flat border. Slugs will chew the leaves but rarely kill established clumps; divide every 3-4 years in July or August when bloom drops off.

3a.notes: Slugs chew emerging fans and flower stalks in our wet PNW spring. Reapply after heavy rain.

Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers — they promote rhizome rot in our wet clay soils. Scratch lightly into soil around (not on) the rhizome. Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
A second light feeding after blooming supports next year's flower buds forming in the rhizome.
Remove individual spent flowers, then cut the entire stalk to the base once all buds finish. Prevents energy waste on seed pods.

3a.product: Trowel + sharp knife; dust cuts with cinnamon or sulfur
3a.notes:
Watch for: rhizome rot and borers
Soft, smelly rhizomes are bacterial rot — common in PNW clay. Cut out all mushy tissue back to firm flesh and let air-dry.
Cut fans to 4–6 inches and remove all dead leaves to reduce overwintering fungal spores and slug habitat through wet winter.
Divide every 3–4 years when blooms decline or rhizomes crowd. Mid-summer dry spell is ideal — replant with tops of rhizomes exposed to sun, never buried.

3a.product: Coarse grit or pumice; remove heavy mulch
3a.notes: In heavy clay, work grit into planting area and keep mulch away from rhizomes. Standing winter water is the #1 killer of bearded iris here.

3a.product: Evergreen boughs or loose straw (4–6 inches)
3a.notes: Apply AFTER ground freezes to prevent frost heaving in extreme cold. Use loose, airy material that won't trap moisture against rhizomes. Remove promptly in spring as soon as snow melts.
Pull mulch off as soon as snow recedes and soil begins to thaw — leaving it on traps moisture and rots rhizomes.

3b.notes: Slugs chew emerging fans and flower stalks in our wet PNW spring. Reapply after heavy rain.

Espoma Bloom! 1-3-1 liquid is a good supplement during bloom — alternate with the main fertilizer every 2 weeks while the plant is actively flowering. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.
A second light feeding after blooming supports next year's flower buds forming in the rhizome.
Remove individual spent flowers, then cut the entire stalk to the base once all buds finish. Prevents energy waste on seed pods.

Watch for: rhizome rot and borers
Soft, smelly rhizomes are bacterial rot. Cut out all mushy tissue back to firm flesh and let air-dry.
Cut fans to 4–6 inches and remove all dead/yellowing leaves to reduce fungal spores and slug habitat.

Divide every 3–4 years when blooms decline or rhizomes crowd. Fall after heat breaks is ideal — replant with tops of rhizomes exposed to sun, never buried. Note: bearded iris struggle here without a cool winter; consider Louisiana or 'reblooming Southern' varieties.

10a.notes: Hot afternoon sun can cook rhizomes here. Filtered or morning-only sun preserves plants — but they still need at least 6 hours direct light for bloom.

10b.notes: Slugs chew emerging fans and flower stalks. Reapply after heavy rain.

Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers — they promote rhizome rot. Scratch lightly into soil around (not on) the rhizome. Low nitrogen, high phosphorus = more flowers, not more leaves.

Remove individual spent flowers, then cut the entire stalk to the base once all buds finish. Prevents energy waste on seed pods.

10b.product: Trowel + sharp knife; dust cuts with cinnamon or sulfur
10b.notes:
Watch for: rhizome rot and borers
Soft, smelly rhizomes are bacterial rot. Year-round warmth and humidity make rot a constant risk. Cut out all mushy tissue back to firm flesh and let air-dry.

Divide every 3–4 years when blooms decline or rhizomes crowd. Late fall after heat breaks is ideal — replant with tops of rhizomes exposed to sun, never buried. Note: bearded iris are marginal here without sufficient winter chill; consider Louisiana iris or chill-tolerant rebloomers as alternatives.

10b.product: Coarse grit or pumice; remove heavy mulch
10b.notes: Work grit into planting area and keep mulch away from rhizomes. Standing water is a major killer of bearded iris.

10b.product: 40–50% shade cloth or sited near taller perennials
10b.notes: Intense afternoon sun cooks rhizomes here. Filtered or morning-only sun is essential — but plants still need at least 6 hours direct light for bloom.
